metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move `overlap.enabled` from the `LLMConfig`/`[tool.rebar.llm]` layer into `VerifyConfig` / `[tool.rebar][verify]` (alongside `enable_code_review`, `novelty_drop_active`, etc.) so all plan-review feature gates live in one consistent config section; update the gating check to read `config.load_config(repo_root).verify.overlap_enabled` matching the `_maybe_apply_*` pattern.
findings.finding: `overlap.enabled` is routed to the `rebar.llm` config layer (`LLMConfig` / `[tool.rebar.llm]`) rather than `VerifyConfig` (`[tool.rebar][verify]`), where every analogous plan-review feature gate already lives — proliferating config surface across two distinct layers for the same category of toggle.
findings.location: ## Config section: 'overlap.enabled in rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_enabled: bool = False` to `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` and register `'overlap_enabled': lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` in `_SECTIONS['verify']`. The `_maybe_apply_overlap` guard then reads `_config.load_config(repo_root).verify.overlap_enabled`, exactly mirroring `_maybe_apply_rising_floor`'s `verify.novelty_drop_active` check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `overlap.enabled` belongs in `VerifyConfig` (src/rebar/_config_schema.py, alongside `remediation_mode`, `progressive_drift_refresh`, etc.) rather than `LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm]`, matching the established pattern for all plan-review feature flags.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'overlap.enabled' belongs to the 'rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])'. Every analogous gate feature flag (remediation_mode, progressive_drift_refresh, novelty_drop_active, completion_floor_active) lives in VerifyConfig in src/rebar/_config_schema.py — not in LLMConfig. Placing overlap.enabled in LLMConfig would break the established pattern and diverge from how all other plan-review feature flags are read (via load_config(repo_root).verify.<flag>).
findings.location: Scope / Config section: 'overlap.enabled in rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_enabled: bool = False` to VerifyConfig in src/rebar/_config_schema.py (not LLMConfig), then read it as `load_config(repo_root).verify.overlap_enabled` inside _run_plan_review — consistent with how remediation_mode, progressive_drift_refresh, and completion_floor_active are read.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact config edit target: either add an `overlap_enabled: bool = False` field directly to `LLMConfig` (with resolution in `from_env()` via `_read_llm_file_table`) or define a new `OverlapConfig` sub-dataclass analogous to `LangfuseConfig`, and name both the field addition and the `from_env()` wiring in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan names 'overlap.enabled in rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])' as an edit target, but LLMConfig (src/rebar/llm/config.py:435) has no overlap field and from_env() does not resolve any overlap.* key from the file table or environment. There is no existing overlap section, sub-dataclass, or resolution path — this is a missing/hallucinated edit target that requires non-trivial new wiring (new field on LLMConfig, or a new sub-config object, plus resolution in from_env()).
findings.location: Scope section: 'overlap.enabled in rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])'; src/rebar/llm/config.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Add `overlap_enabled: bool = False` to `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, resolved from `[tool.rebar.llm].overlap_enabled` via `_llm_bool` (new helper mirroring `_llm_int`) in `from_env()`; env knob `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_ENABLED`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change `overlap.enabled` to `verify.overlap_enabled` in `VerifyConfig` (mirroring `enable_code_review`, `novelty_drop_active`, etc.) and update the Scope section to name `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as the edit target instead of `LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm]`.
findings.finding: The plan routes `overlap.enabled` to `LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm]`, but all analogous feature-flag booleans (`enable_code_review`, `progressive_drift_refresh`, `remediation_mode`, `novelty_drop_active`, `completion_floor_active`) live in `VerifyConfig` under `[tool.rebar]`, registered in `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `_SECTIONS` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`. Placing `overlap.enabled` in `LLMConfig` is inconsistent with the established pattern and would require special-casing.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'overlap.enabled in rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_enabled: bool = False` to `VerifyConfig` and register `'overlap_enabled': lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` in `_SECTIONS['verify']`. Read it in `_run_plan_review` with `_config.load_config(repo_root).verify.overlap_enabled`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `overlap.enabled` belongs in `VerifyConfig` (consistent with all analogous plan-review toggles) or `LLMConfig`, and specify the exact field name, dataclass location, `_SECTIONS` coercer entry, and `from_env`/`from_mapping` loading path.
findings.finding: `overlap.enabled` is assigned to `LLMConfig` / `[tool.rebar.llm]`, but LLMConfig has no overlap field and its from_env() reads only a fixed set of keys. Every analogous plan-review opt-in flag (enable_code_review, progressive_drift_refresh, remediation_mode, completion_floor_active, novelty_drop_active) lives in VerifyConfig / _config_schema.py `_SECTIONS['verify']`. No `overlap` section or dataclass exists in `_config_schema.py` or `_SECTION_CLASSES`. The plan provides no description of how `overlap.enabled` would be loaded, coerced, or accessed at runtime.
findings.location: Scope section ('overlap.enabled in rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])'); src/rebar/llm/config.py:435; src/rebar/_config_schema.py:407-418
findings.suggested_fix: Move overlap.enabled to VerifyConfig as a boolean field (consistent with enable_code_review, remediation_mode, etc.) and add it to _SECTIONS['verify'] with a lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k) coercer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate with the schema and renderer spec a processing protocol that explicitly defines: (a) when overlap[] key is absent (config-off) — render nothing / omit from MCP; (b) when overlap[] is present and empty (runtime error or no candidates) — render a brief note; (c) when overlap[] has items — render each item.
findings.finding: The key-absent vs key-present-but-empty distinction for `overlap[]` is specified as a behavioral rule ('overlap.enabled=off → silent no-op, no overlap[] key' vs 'S1/S5/S6 runtime failure → empty overlap[]') but no processing protocol is co-located with the schema or the CLI renderer/MCP output spec to tell consumers how to handle the absent-key case. The `_render_plan_review_text` and MCP result update descriptions do not specify what to render or return when overlap[] is absent (config-off) versus present-and-empty (runtime error).
findings.location: What section (LLM-gate / graceful skip paragraph); AC3 ('silent no-op, verdict identical' vs 'empty overlap[], logged'); _render_plan_review_text update description

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC line 2 and the What section exactly which text representation of the ticket is passed to S1's digest function (e.g. `ctx.plan_text` = title+description, or a richer field), and confirm this matches the representation used to build the enriched corpus in sibling stories S2/S3/S4.
findings.finding: The plan's AC line 2 ('The query digest is generated at review time from the ticket's current full text') does not specify which representation of 'full text' is passed to S1. In the codebase, `ctx.plan_text` = `title + '\n\n' + description` (det_floor.py:121-122) — it excludes children. For store-wide overlap detection, the digest may need to cover a richer context (e.g. including children's summaries). Without specifying the field, the implementer must guess and S1's digest may be too narrow or misaligned with how similar tickets are digested in the corpus.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observable signal (e.g., a structured log counter or lightweight metric) for overlap-step failures so operators can detect when the step is silently degrading (distinct from legitimately returning zero results).
findings.finding: The plan specifies that S1/S5/S6 failures are 'logged' but introduces no metric, counter, or alert for the overlap step's failure rate, leaving systematic silent failures (e.g., S5 retrieval always timing out) invisible to operators.
findings.location: LLM-gate / graceful skip section: 'caught → empty overlap[], logged, never block'
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a structured log entry with a stable key (e.g., `overlap_step_failed=True, reason=<exception_type>`) on each caught exception so it can be queried/alerted on in log aggregation, distinguishing failure from empty-result.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify renderer behavior when `overlap[]` is absent or empty: either silently omit the section, or define what minimal text is shown (e.g., omit if absent/empty, so default runs produce no extra noise).
findings.finding: The CLI renderer update (`_render_plan_review_text`) is specified to 'print the `overlap[]` section' but does not define what to render when `overlap[]` is absent or empty (e.g., graceful-skip or disabled path). An implementer could reasonably render a blank section header, producing confusing CLI output for the common case where overlap is disabled or skipped.
findings.location: What / Steps bullet: `_render_plan_review_text` (`src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:386`); Testing bullet: '`overlap[]` appears in both CLI text and MCP JSON'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'the `overlap[]` section is omitted from CLI text output (and from the MCP result key) when the array is absent or empty, so default/graceful-skip runs produce no UI change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that S6's judge output schema constrains `relation` to a subset of `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` (e.g. `duplicates | supersedes | depends_on | relates_to`) and add a post-judge normalization/validation step that maps or rejects out-of-vocabulary relations before constructing the `rebar link` command.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that each `overlap[]` item carries a `rebar link` command, implying the pairwise judge (S6) emits a relation name. The live `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` in `src/rebar/graph/_links.py:19-23` accepts exactly 6 values (`blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`); `rebar link` raises `ValueError` on any other string. The plan never asserts that S6's judge output schema is constrained to this closed set, creating a risk that the judge emits a relation (e.g. `similar_to`, `overlaps_with`, or a paraphrase) that causes the surfaced `rebar link` command to fail when the user runs it.
findings.location: ## What — 'each item = the S6 finding incl. its rebar link command' / AC1
findings.suggested_fix: In the S6 judge schema, enumerate the allowed relations as a JSON enum drawn directly from `CANONICAL_RELATIONS`; add a one-line guard in the integration that skips any item whose relation is not in `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` and logs a warning.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `rebar link` relation the S6 judge should embed in each overlap finding (e.g. `relates_to` or `duplicates`) and add it to the `overlap[]` item schema definition.
findings.finding: The `rebar link` command requires a mandatory `<relation>` argument (blocks|depends_on|relates_to|duplicates|supersedes|discovered_from), but the plan never specifies which relation the S6 judge should emit in each overlap item's `rebar link` command string, making the output undefined and potentially syntactically invalid.
findings.location: ## What — 'each item = the S6 finding incl. its rebar link command'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `relates_to` as the default relation (it is the semantically neutral 'these tickets are related' choice) and specify the command format as `rebar link <query_ticket_id> <candidate_ticket_id> relates_to` in the overlap item schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving test (e.g. `test_digest_from_fulltext`) that asserts the query digest is derived from the ticket's current full text at review time, specifically with a ticket NOT present in the enriched corpus, and reference it in AC2.
findings.finding: AC2 ('The query digest is generated at review time from the ticket's current full text') has no mapped unit test or proving command anywhere in the plan; the Testing section lists four tests but none explicitly asserts digest-source behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; Testing section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section add: '`test_digest_from_fulltext`: pass a ticket absent from the fixture corpus; assert `overlap[]` is non-empty (judge still ran) and that the digest fed to S5 matches `hashlib.sha256(current_full_text.encode()).hexdigest()`.' Update AC2 to cite this test as its proving command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py` (the `review_plan` tool docstring at line 159) to the Scope edit list, explicitly calling out that the result-key enumeration in the docstring must include `overlap[]`.
findings.finding: The Scope edit-set does not enumerate the MCP review_plan tool docstring (src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py:158-174) as a consumer requiring update. The docstring explicitly lists the result dict keys ('{verdict: ..., blocking[], advisory[], coaching[], indeterminate[], coverage, signature?, source, verified_at_sha, ...}') and adding overlap[] to the result without updating this docstring leaves the MCP tool's self-description stale.
findings.location: Scope section (edit list); src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py:158-174

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prior-art section explaining why `scan_epics_for_spec`/`spec_alignment` and the G4 scope-overlap mode are insufficient for the store-wide overlap use case (e.g. scalability to large corpus, need for digest-based recall, synchronous integration inside `_run_plan_review`), so the novel pipeline is justified rather than assumed.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new digest→retrieve→judge overlap pipeline without acknowledging or ruling out existing codebase prior art that already detects store-wide scope overlap: `scan_epics_for_spec` (`src/rebar/llm/spec_scan.py`) evaluates open epics for scope overlaps against an arbitrary spec text; the `spec_alignment` reviewer (`src/rebar/llm/reviewers/spec_alignment.md`) explicitly detects 'scope overlaps: epics that duplicate each other's scope'; and the `plan_review_G4` criterion already fires a scope-overlap mode during plan review. None of these mechanisms are mentioned, and no argument is made for why they are insufficient for the store-wide overlap use case.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What / ## Dependencies
findings.suggested_fix: Document in ## Why or a new ## Prior Art section: 'scan_epics_for_spec is a batch async op unsuitable for inline synchronous use inside _run_plan_review; G4 covers only same-parent children; spec_alignment requires an explicit spec text and does not scale to a store-wide corpus retrieval. The digest+BM25F+judge stack is chosen because [X].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the timing rationale: state that overlap[] is appended to the verdict dict AFTER `gate_dispatch.produce_plan_review_verdict()` returns (where `coverage['counts']` is already frozen), before the sidecar emit and signing — and that the separate `overlap[]` key is what guarantees counts/attestation are unaffected, not injection ordering.
findings.finding: The plan's timing claim 'inject overlap findings BEFORE coverage[counts] is baked' is factually wrong: coverage['counts'] is assembled inside gate_dispatch.produce_plan_review_verdict() and is already frozen when the overlap injection point runs. An implementer following this rationale may attempt to insert the overlap step before produce_plan_review_verdict (the wrong site), defeating the plan's own stated 'chosen approach' of a separate overlap[] key appended after verdict production.
findings.location: What section — 'inject overlap findings BEFORE coverage[counts] is baked'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'BEFORE coverage[counts] is baked' with 'AFTER produce_plan_review_verdict returns (coverage[counts] is already frozen at this point); the separate overlap[] key is the mechanism that keeps counts and attestation unaffected, not timing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the sequencing contradiction: remove the 'inject BEFORE coverage[counts] is baked' language (it is architecturally impossible post-produce_plan_review_verdict) and state definitively that overlap[] is appended to the verdict dict after produce_plan_review_verdict returns, is excluded from coverage['counts'], and does not affect the signed manifest.
findings.finding: The plan's sequencing protocol for injecting overlap findings is self-contradictory: it first states injection must happen 'BEFORE coverage[counts] is baked' so advisory_surfaced and the signed manifest include them, then immediately pivots to 'chosen approach: a separate overlap[] key excluded from the advisory cap AND from the signed coverage counts.' But `coverage['counts']` is assembled *inside* `produce_plan_review_verdict` (orchestrator.py:500), which is called and returned at `_run_plan_review` line 434 — the overlap injection site is necessarily AFTER counts are baked. An executor cannot determine whether overlap findings should influence coverage['counts'] and the signed manifest or not.
findings.location: What section ('inject overlap findings BEFORE coverage[counts] is baked…OR, explicitly, keep overlap findings in a SEPARATE overlap[] verdict key excluded from the advisory cap and from the signed coverage counts'); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:434; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:500

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default field names for each `overlap[]` item in the plan (e.g. `matched_ticket_id`, `summary`, `similarity_score`, `rebar_link`) so the executor can write the JSON schema and renderer without guessing.
findings.finding: The exact JSON field names for each item in `overlap[]` are unspecified — the plan says only 'the S6 finding incl. its `rebar link` command', leaving the executor to invent field names when editing `plan_review_verdict.schema.json`.
findings.location: What section — 'each item = the S6 finding incl. its `rebar link` command'; Scope — `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_verdict.schema.json`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under 'What': 'Each `overlap[]` item is a JSON object with fields: `matched_ticket` (string, ticket ID/title), `summary` (string, one-sentence judge rationale), `score` (number, 0–1), `rebar_link` (string, the `rebar link <id>` command). These map directly to the S6 judge output fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `overlap[]` item field vocabulary (e.g., `candidate_ticket_id`, `similarity_score`, `finding`, `rebar_link`) in `plan_review_verdict.schema.json` and reference it from the MCP output schema and CLI renderer spec.
findings.finding: The `overlap[]` array item schema is never defined: the plan says each item equals 'the S6 finding incl. its rebar link command' but specifies no field vocabulary (e.g., candidate_ticket_id, similarity_score, finding text, rebar_link command string). The plan_review_verdict.schema.json has no `overlap` property, and no sibling schema defines this structure. An implementer cannot produce a consistent, validated item shape.
findings.location: What section ('each item = the S6 finding incl. its rebar link command'); src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_verdict.schema.json (missing overlap property)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `overlap` array property to `plan_review_verdict.schema.json` with an `items` schema defining at minimum: `candidate_ticket_id` (string), `finding` (string), `rebar_link` (string — the exact `rebar link <id1> <id2> <relation>` command), and optionally `similarity_score` (number).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose the overlap result cap (currently hardcoded at 3) as a configurable value (e.g., `overlap.max_results` in the `[tool.rebar.llm]` config layer alongside `overlap.enabled`) so it can be tuned without a code change.
findings.finding: The ≤3 overlap-item cap is hardcoded in the design with no config knob, making it a hardcoded threshold expected to evolve (different teams/personas may want more or fewer items) that requires a code change to tune.
findings.location: What section: '≤3 results', '≤3 overlap items never displace real plan-review advisories under the cap'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap.max_results: int = 3` to the typed `LLMConfig` (alongside `overlap.enabled`) and thread it through `_run_plan_review` so the cap is read from config rather than hardcoded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the injection-point description: `coverage['counts']` is baked inside `orchestrator.produce_plan_review_verdict()` before `_run_plan_review` receives the verdict, so the injection point is always post-orchestrator (after line 434) and pre-sidecar (before line 465) — the separate `overlap[]` key design already accommodates this correctly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that overlap findings should be injected 'BEFORE coverage["counts"] is baked'. However, in the actual code, coverage['counts'] is assembled inside orchestrator.produce_plan_review_verdict() (orchestrator.py:500-506), which is called atomically at __init__.py:434. By the time _run_plan_review receives the verdict dict, counts are already baked. The stated pre-baking injection point does not exist as a separable step in _run_plan_review.
findings.location: What section: 'inject overlap findings BEFORE coverage["counts"] is baked and BEFORE the sidecar emit + attestation signing'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'BEFORE coverage["counts"] is baked' with 'after gate_dispatch.produce_plan_review_verdict() returns and before the sidecar emit (lines 434–465)' — the separate overlap[] key means counts are unaffected regardless.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile 'Why' language: replace 'advisory coaching' with wording that makes clear overlap findings go into a dedicated `overlap[]` key outside the advisory stream (e.g. 'surfaced as a separate `overlap[]` section, not counted as plan-review advisories').
findings.finding: The 'Why' section describes overlap findings as 'advisory coaching' folded into 'the plan-review output', implying they are advisories; but the 'What' section explicitly states the chosen approach puts them in a SEPARATE `overlap[]` key 'excluded from the advisory cap' and 'never displace real plan-review advisories.' AC5 confirms the separate key. An implementer reading 'Why' first could wire the findings into the advisory stream rather than the separate key.
findings.location: Why section vs What section (chosen approach parenthetical) vs AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Why' to: 'fold its results into the plan-review output as a dedicated `overlap[]` section (separate from and never counted against the advisory cap).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or CLAUDE.md section) documenting the architectural decision: why `overlap[]` is a separate verdict key, why its cap is independent of the advisory cap, and why the attestation/coverage counts must be byte-identical regardless of overlap results.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel pipeline stage (overlap check embedded in plan-review with a separate verdict key, a separate cap, and a byte-identical attestation guarantee) but does not mention capturing this architectural decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update.
findings.location: Scope section; What section (chosen approach rationale)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit note acknowledging that `overlap[]` is additive/optional in the verdict schema and confirming backward compatibility for existing MCP consumers and verdict-parsing callers (or list any callers that need updating).
findings.finding: The plan adds `overlap[]` to `plan_review_verdict.schema.json` and to the MCP `review_plan` output model, but does not acknowledge existing MCP consumers or downstream callers that parse the verdict JSON. While adding an optional key is usually backward-compatible, the schema update is a caller-facing change and E3(d) requires acknowledgment of callers depending on the old behavior.
findings.location: Scope section: '`src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_verdict.schema.json` (add `overlap[]`)', 'MCP `review_plan` output model'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify 'include overlap[] in the MCP review_plan result + its output schema': the MCP `review_plan` tool is NO_SCHEMA_EXEMPT (no FastMCP outputSchema annotation by design); if the intent is only to update `plan_review_verdict.schema.json`, say so explicitly — do not add an outputSchema annotation to the MCP tool without a deliberate policy decision.
findings.finding: The plan states 'include overlap[] in the MCP review_plan result + its output schema'. The MCP review_plan tool is explicitly designated NO_SCHEMA_EXEMPT — it advertises no outputSchema by design (documented in src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py:174 and src/rebar/_mcp_models.py:162). Updating 'its output schema' conflicts with this established and intentional exemption.
findings.location: What section / AC line 4: 'include overlap[] in the MCP review_plan result + its output schema'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope (or a new 'Interface Contract' subsection) the expected module paths and call signatures for S1, S5, and S6 (e.g. `from rebar.llm.overlap.digest import extract_digest`, `from rebar.llm.overlap.retrieve import retrieve`, `from rebar.llm.overlap.judge import judge`) so the _run_plan_review integration edit is fully self-contained.
findings.finding: The Scope edit-set never names the module paths for S1 (enrich/extract), S5 (retrieve), and S6 (judge) from which _run_plan_review will import. These are described as 'prerequisite sibling stories' whose components must already exist when this story lands, but without specifying import paths (e.g. from rebar.llm.overlap import extract_digest, retrieve, judge), the integration site edit in _run_plan_review is under-specified as an edit target.
findings.location: Scope section / What section 'run retrieve (S5), run the pairwise judge (S6)'; Dependencies section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the `_run_plan_review` injection site: explain that `cfg`, `runner`, and the already-assembled `ctx` are available there, that it follows the same established pattern as the novelty-floor and completion-floor steps, and that it runs inside the `gate_read_root` snapshot session required by S1/S5/S6.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for why the `_run_plan_review` function at :401 is the correct injection site rather than, for example, the public `review_plan` wrapper at :352. The distinction is only expressed negatively ('NOT the public wrapper'). The absence of a positive rationale leaves implementers without the reasoning needed to evaluate future injection-site decisions (e.g. if a new gate boundary is introduced).
findings.location: What section — 'Integrate in _run_plan_review (:401) ... NOT the public wrapper review_plan at :352'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the BM25F library (or retrieval mechanism) S5 will provide and confirm it is (or will be) in the project's dependency set; add an import-availability guard to the integration's LLM-gate / graceful-skip path so a missing library logs a clear diagnostic rather than a silent empty overlap[].
findings.finding: The plan never names a Python library that provides BM25F retrieval (referenced in sibling story S5, on which this plan depends via 'run retrieve (S5)'). No BM25F implementation exists anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by search), and the plan provides no fallback if the library is unavailable. This is an unverified capability assertion whose resolution is deferred entirely to S5 — but the integration plan (this story) provides no acceptance check that S5's interface and the library are actually available before wiring them into `_run_plan_review`.
findings.location: ## What — 'run retrieve (S5)' / ## Dependencies

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the callable API contract (module path, function signature, return type, raised exception types) for the S1 digest extractor, S5 retrieve function, and S6 judge function — either in this plan or in a shared interface spec referenced here — so the injection code and FakeRunner fixtures can be written against a stable contract.
findings.finding: S1/S5/S6 (enrich, retrieve, judge) callables are not defined anywhere in the codebase; their function signatures, module paths, and exception types are left entirely unspecified in this plan, making it impossible to verify the integration is achievable as described or to write the unit tests without further design.
findings.location: ## Dependencies / ## What (S1/S5/S6 integration steps)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Interface contract' subsection to ## What specifying at minimum: `from rebar.llm.overlap import extract_digest, retrieve_candidates, judge_pair`; the return type of each (e.g. `extract_digest(ctx) -> str`, `retrieve_candidates(digest, *, cfg, repo_root) -> list[dict]`, `judge_pair(ticket_ctx, candidate, *, cfg, runner) -> dict | None`); and the single exception type the caller catches (e.g. `OverlapError`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an interface contract stub for S1/S5/S6 to this story: specify the module path, function signature (inputs, return type), and exception types each raises, so the integration error-handling wrapper can be correctly scoped.
findings.finding: The S1 (digest extraction), S5 (retrieve), and S6 (pairwise judge) sub-step interfaces are entirely undefined in this integration plan. The plan calls them synchronously ('synchronously extract the query ticket's digest from the already-assembled full-text context (S1), run retrieve (S5), run the pairwise judge (S6)') but specifies no input parameters, return types, or error contract for any of them. The codebase has zero matches for these components (searches for 'ticket_digest', 'cupid', 'def retrieve' in LLM pipeline, and pairwise judge return no results). The plan marks them as OUT of scope but relies on calling them with specific outputs.
findings.location: What section (S1/S5/S6 step sequence); Dependencies section ('S1 (enrich), S5 (retrieve), S6 (judge). These are prerequisite sibling stories…'); Scope section ('OUT: the component ops (S1/S5/S6)')

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
